metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Seth Brown — Humanitarians AI Fellow. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Kent documents how credit repeatedly detaches from the people who actually did the execution. At Atari, Bushnell had the idea, Alcorn built it, and Bushnell became the legend. This isn't about prior invention in some abstract historical sense; it's about who gets remembered. Kent traces this same credit-consolidation pattern across three decades of the industry.

artifactLines: Kent documents how credit repeatedly detaches from the people who actually did the execution.
At Atari, Bushnell had the idea, Alcorn built it, and Bushnell became the legend.
This isn't about prior invention in some abstract historical sense; it's about who gets remembered.
Kent traces this same credit-consolidation pattern across three decades of the industry.

beats.narration_text: The Atari split you described (Bushnell's idea, Alcorn's execution, Bushnell's legend) captures how credit detaches from the work. I want to see the pattern Kent traces across three decades. Help me: (1) define the real difference between having the idea and doing the execution, (2) explain the mechanism by which the 'legend' consolidates onto one name, and (3) give me one modern tech example where the same detachment happened.
